prophecy-and-persia: Welcome to Prophecy and Persia: Cyrus, Islam and the Imam Mahdi. In front of you stands the towering stone gateway of Persepolis, its colossal winged bull-men keeping watch as they have for millennia. This exhibition, presented by the Department for Outreach and Public Relations of the Ahmadiyya Muslim Community UK, follows a single remarkable thread through history, in three parts. First, we meet Cyrus the Great, the Persian emperor whom the Holy Quran may call Dhul-Qarnain. Then we witness the Rise of Islam and Persia's place within it. Finally, we come to the Promised Messiah, the awaited reformer of Persian origin, the Imam Mahdi. The artefacts you will encounter along the way are courtesy of Razwan Baig and the Barlas Foundation. This audio guide will accompany you at every board, and is available in several languages. Take your time, and let the journey begin.
cyrus: Cyrus. You are entering Section One, and its purpose is to unravel the true identity of Dhul-Qarnain, the two-horned king mentioned in the Holy Quran. Before you is the carved face of a Persian noble, gazing out from the stone as it has for two and a half thousand years. Over the boards ahead, you will meet Cyrus the Great, founder of the Persian Empire, and weigh the case that he, rather than Alexander, was the righteous figure the Quran describes. Watch for his conquests to the west, the east and the north, for his famous cylinder, and for the wall he raised against Gog and Magog. Let us begin with the promise of the Holy Quran itself.
promise-of-the-holy-quran: The Promise of the Holy Quran. Shown here in flowing Arabic calligraphy, the Holy Quran is unique. It tells us that God Almighty is the Lord of every realm; of every people, and every nation. This means that no one people is inherently supreme over any others; all human beings are equal before God. Our division into different peoples is not a hierarchy among humanity. Rather, those closest to Allah are simply those who are most righteous. But how can we become righteous? Through following the Prophets. For Islam teaches that all humanity received God's guidance. As the Quran declares, in chapter sixteen, verse thirty-seven: "And We did raise among every people a Messenger with the teaching, 'Worship Allah and shun the Evil One.' Then among them were some whom Allah guided and among them were some who became deserving of ruin." Each people, then, was sent a Prophet; yet over time, people corrupted their religions. At last the time came when humanity could bear a single, universal message: Islam, a universal message for all peoples and all time. And the Quran claims explicitly to be that universal teaching to unite all humanity, declaring in chapter seven, verse one hundred and fifty-nine: "Say, 'O mankind! truly I am a Messenger to you all from Allah to Whom belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth. There is no God but He. He gives life, and He causes death...'" This message was revealed through the Prophet Muhammad, peace and blessings be upon him, who was born in five hundred and ninety, received his first revelation of the Quran in six hundred and ten, and saw the Quran completed by six hundred and thirty-two. The message of Islam is simple, and should its spirit ever be lost by the Muslims themselves, Allah promised to rejuvenate it by His own hand: "Verily, We Ourself have sent down this Exhortation, and most surely We will be its Guardian" — chapter fifteen, verse ten. And the story of how this renewal came to pass is inimitably tied up with one particular people: the Persians.
ancient-persia-zoroastrianism: Ancient Persia and Zoroastrianism. In front of you rise the ruins of ancient Persepolis at golden hour. First, let us place Persia and Iran in history. The name 'Persia' comes from the Ancient Persian word 'Parsa', the name for what is now the Iranian province of Fars. The inhabitants of this area called themselves the Arya, meaning 'the noble' or 'the civilised', and they called their homeland 'Iran'. Fars, or Parsa, was the centre of Cyrus the Great's Achaemenid Dynasty; and when the Greeks came across this empire, they called their realm Persis, in Latin, Persia. As the Persian Empire spread, the name 'Persians' became applicable to a huge number of peoples living under Persian rule. As for dates: linguistic analysis of the Gathas places Zoroaster in the second millennium before the Common Era, between twelve hundred and one thousand BCE; Greek historians, however, give the sixth century BCE as his birth century; and six hundred BCE is the birth year of Cyrus the Second, the Great. Long before Cyrus, then, came the Prophet Zoroaster. Zarathustra, later known as Zoroaster, was a great prophetic figure in the Iranian tradition. His society worshipped many gods and brutalised their neighbours. Zarathustra was commissioned by Ahura Mazda, the One True God, to spiritually reform his people, and he taught monotheism. In the Gathas, the Zoroastrian scriptures, he proclaimed, as recorded in Yasna forty-five, verse six: "Now, I shall speak of the Greatest One of all, praising with truth Him who is beneficent through His virtuous spirit to those who exist." In the same scriptures, Zarathustra recalls Ahura Mazda questioning him, in Yasna forty-three, verses seven to eight: "How, this day, wouldst thou begin to explain these revelations among thy creatures and thine own?" Then I said to Him first, "I am Zarathustra. If I were able, I would be a true enemy to the deceitful one but a strong support to the Truthful One." Yet he was bitterly opposed by the priests and princes of his land, and was forced to migrate for his faith. In that hour he lamented, in Yasna forty-six, verse one: "To what land shall I turn, and whither turning shall I go? For my kinsmen and my peers have deserted me. Not the people, nor their wicked rulers, favor me. How shall I satisfy Thee, O Ahura Mazda?" In the end, though, he was victorious. His spiritual kingdom became a worldly kingdom through one of the greatest sons of Persia: Cyrus the Great.
cyrus-ii-persian-emperor: Cyrus the Second, Persian Emperor. This carved relief from Persepolis shows a procession of Persian and Median nobles. Cyrus was born into a world on fire. Between nine hundred and eleven and six hundred and nine BCE, the regional superpower was the Neo-Assyrian empire. They were famous for their viciousness, and dominated the Medes of northwestern Iran and the Babylonians of Southern Mesopotamia. In seven hundred and twenty-two BCE the Assyrians captured the Northern Kingdom of Israel and enslaved them, the so-called lost tribes; and in six hundred and twelve BCE the Assyrians themselves were destroyed by a coalition of their enemies, as the Babylonians united with the Medians and the Scythians to conquer them. The Babylonians then inherited the Kingdom of Judah in Jerusalem. When the Israelites rebelled against their new King, he crushed them, destroying Solomon's Temple around five hundred and eighty-seven BCE. All of this had been foretold. After the Assyrian enslavement of the Northern Kingdom of Israel, the Prophet Nahum foretold Assyria's downfall, saying, in the Book of Nahum, chapter one, verse fourteen: "You will have no descendants to bear your name. I will destroy the images and idols that are in the temple of your gods. I will prepare your grave, for you are vile." His prophecy was fulfilled within decades. But the Babylonians then captured the Southern Kingdom of Judah, and exiled its people, who wept in captivity. As the Psalm records, in Psalm one hundred and thirty-seven, verse one: "By the rivers of Babylon, there we sat down, yea, we wept, when we remembered Zion." Their saviour would not be an Israelite, but a Zoroastrian; and the Bible would call him 'Messiah'. Into this turmoil came Cyrus, born around six hundred to five hundred and ninety BCE. In five hundred and fifty-nine BCE he inherited the kingdom of the small Persian state of Anshan, a vassal of the Medians led by a man who was potentially Cyrus's own grandfather, Astyages. Astyages was said to be a cruel ruler, whose policies bred great disaffection among the Median nobility. Cyrus overthrew him and united the kingdoms of Media and Persia under himself, and in doing so laid the foundation of the great Achaemenid Empire. Yet it would be the Holy Quran that would forever establish his divine support, through the enigmatic story of Dhul-Qarnain: the Two-Horned King.
the-cyrus-cylinder: The Cyrus Cylinder. Behind you stands a replica of the Cyrus Cylinder, inscribed around five hundred and thirty-nine BCE. In Cyrus's own voice it declares: "I am Cyrus, King of the world... When I entered Babylon, I did not allow anyone to terrorise the land. I strove for peace in Babylon and in all her cities... Marduk, the great lord, rejoiced at my good deeds, and he pronounced a sweet blessing over me, Cyrus, the king who fears him, and over Cambyses, the son my issue, and over all my troops, that we might live happily in his presence, in well-being." The cylinder records the conquest of Babylon in the native Akkadian tongue. Its first part is written in the third person, and presents Cyrus as the chosen servant of Marduk, Babylon's 'lord of all gods', sent to punish their previous leader, Nabonidus. The text then switches to a first-person account in the voice of Cyrus, which describes his being chosen by Marduk to enter Babylon in peace. It explains how he treated the people of Babylon with kindness, and how he restored the gods to their temples, so that they could be worshipped by their respective devotees. The text is significant, for it shows how Cyrus's conquest was received and understood by the Babylonian elite themselves, and it stands as one of the earliest surviving records of Cyrus's pioneering religious tolerance.
who-was-dhul-qarnain: Who Was Dhul-Qarnain? Here two ancient maps sit side by side: the empire of Alexander the Great, and the Achaemenid Persian Empire around five hundred BCE. Let us set the story in context. The eighteenth chapter of the Holy Quran outlines the evolution of the Christian world; from the righteous and persecuted followers of the Messiah, to great Christian powers falling prey to materialism. This story comes towards its end when it explains how Dhul-Qarnain stopped the entry of the marauding peoples of Gog and Magog from entering his lands. It would be the descendants of Gog and Magog who would become the great European powers of the Latter Days, colonising the world materially and spiritually. But who, then, was Dhul-Qarnain? Most Muslims believe he was Alexander the Great; the Caliphs of the Ahmadiyya Muslim Community, however, have proved that he was Cyrus the Great. Consider three clues. First, the title 'Dhul-Qarnain' means one who possesses two horns, or who spans two generations, two centuries. Cyrus unified two great kingdoms, the Median and the Persian, and his life spanned two generations, while Alexander died at the young age of just thirty-two. Second, the three journeys of Dhul-Qarnain line up with Cyrus's known conquests, which went west, then east; Alexander's conquests spread only east. As the map of Alexander's empire shows, his conquests began in Macedonia, near Greece, and expanded eastwards only, across Asia Minor, the Near East, and through into India and Afghanistan; he never had any westward or northern expansion to his empire. Cyrus, by contrast, rose to kingship in the fortieth year of his life. His first conquest was in five hundred and fifty BCE, of the Medes. He then proceeded westward, conquering Turkey in five hundred and forty-six BCE, before heading east to conquer through to Uzbekistan; and finally, the Quran indicates, he secured his northern border at the Caucasus mountains. And third, Cyrus the Great was a magnificent moral and religious figure, ushering in an unprecedented era of religious freedom. Alexander was a brilliant but profligate pagan conqueror; he cannot be described as the righteous figure of Dhul-Qarnain. The evidence, then, points firmly to Cyrus.
the-story-of-dhul-qarnain: The Story of Dhul-Qarnain. Here is the Quranic account itself, taken from the Holy Quran, chapter eighteen, verses eighty-five to one hundred. Take a moment as we read it in full. “And they ask thee about Dhul-Qarnain. Say, ‘I will certainly recite to you something of his story. We established him in the earth and gave him the means to accomplish everything. Then he followed a certain way. Until, when he reached the setting of the sun, he found it setting in a pool of murky water, and near it he found a people. We said, ‘O Dhul-Qarnain, either punish them, or treat them with kindness.’ He said, ‘As for him who does wrong, we shall certainly punish him; then shall he be brought back to his Lord, Who will punish him with a dreadful punishment, But as for him who believes and acts righteously, he will have a good reward, and We shall speak to him easy words of Our command’. Then indeed he followed another way. Until, when he reached the rising of the sun, he found it rising on a people for whom We had made no shelter against it. Thus indeed it was. Verily, We encompassed with Our knowledge all that was with him. Then he followed another way. Until, when he reached the place between the two mountains, he found beneath them a people who would scarcely understand a word. They said, ‘O Dhul-Qarnain, verily, Gog and Magog are creating disorder in the earth; shall we then pay thee tribute on condition that thou set up a barrier between us and them?’ He replied, ‘The power with which my Lord has endowed me about this is better, but you may help me with physical strength; I will set up between you and them a rampart. Bring me blocks of iron.’ They did so till, when he had levelled up the space between the two mountain sides, he said, ‘Now blow with your bellows.’ They blew with bellows till, when he had made it red as fire, he said, ‘Bring me molten copper that I may pour it thereon.’ So they — Gog and Magog — were not able to scale it, nor were they able to dig through it. Thereupon he said, ‘This is a mercy from my Lord. But when the promise of my Lord shall come to pass, He will break it into pieces. And the promise of my Lord is certainly true.’ And on that day We shall leave some of them to surge against others, and the trumpet will be blown. Then shall We gather them all together.” This single passage, as the exhibition will show, holds the key both to the story of Cyrus and to prophecies still waiting to unfold.
persian-king-israelite-savior: Persian King, Israelite Saviour. This board follows Dhul-Qarnain’s westward journey, and shows how a Persian king became the saviour of the people of Israel. The Holy Quran, chapter eighteen, verse eighty-seven, tells us: “Until, when he reached the setting of the sun, he found it setting in a pool of murky water, and near it he found a people. We said, ‘O Dhu’l Qarnain, either punish them, or treat them with kindness.’” The sun, of course, sets in the west. And it was a king of Western Turkey who attacked Cyrus in five hundred and forty-six BCE. Cyrus marched from his base in Iran and defeated an army twice the size of his own. This region, near today’s Izmir, is famous for its hot mineral springs and muddy pools; even now the ‘mud baths of Turkey’ are a centre of spa tourism, testifying to the Quran’s use of the word ‘ayn hami’ah, which means, in Lane’s lexicon, “a well or a spring or pool foul with black, fetid mud.” It is a vivid vindication of the Quran’s description. Cyrus then turned to Babylon, where the Jews lay in exile, forced to worship idols and robbed of their homeland, Jerusalem. In five hundred and thirty-nine BCE he defeated Babylon’s army at Opis, then took the city in an extraordinary fashion, diverting the River Euphrates away from the city’s water defences so that his men could wade in by night, and the city was conquered without battle. There Cyrus fulfilled the Quranic instruction perfectly, punishing the guilty and showing kindness to others. He punished the Babylonian elite — the Prince Regent Belshazzar was killed on the night of conquest, ending the Chaldean dynasty. He reversed the forced-labour policies the Babylonians had imposed on conquered peoples. He restored the religious temples of all, and decreed the rebuilding of the Temple of Solomon. And he permitted the Israelites to return to Judea and rebuild their communities. Consider the timeline: in five hundred and forty-six BCE Cyrus took Western Turkey; in five hundred and thirty-nine BCE he defeated the Babylonians; and between five hundred and thirty-eight and five hundred and thirty-six BCE, the rebuilding of the Second Temple began. Without Cyrus, the religion of Moses that later came to be known as Judaism would likely not exist today as we know it.
the-prophecy-of-isaiah: The Prophecy of Isaiah. This board sets before you a prophecy spoken long before Cyrus was born, from the Book of Isaiah, chapter forty-five, verses one to six. It names him directly. Listen: “Thus says the Lord to His anointed one — mashiach — to Cyrus, whose right hand I have held, to subdue nations before him and loose the armor of kings, to open before him the double doors, so that the gates will not be shut. ‘I will go before you and make the crooked places straight; I will break in pieces the gates of bronze and cut the bars of iron. I will give you the treasures of darkness and hidden riches of secret places, that you may know that I, the Lord, Who call you by your name, am the God of Israel. For Jacob My servant’s sake, and Israel My elect, I have even called you by your name; I have named you, though you have not known Me. I am the Lord, and there is no other; there is no God besides Me. I will gird you, though you have not known Me, that they may know from the rising of the sun to its setting that there is none besides Me. I am the Lord, and there is no other…’” Note what this scripture declares. The Bible calls Cyrus its anointed one, its mashiach, its Messiah. And God calls him by his very name, and holds his right hand, though Cyrus had not yet come to know Him.
false-claims-of-cyrus-death: False Claims of Cyrus’ Death. This board takes up Dhul-Qarnain’s eastward journey, and answers the old claim that Cyrus perished on that eastern front. The Holy Quran, chapter eighteen, verses ninety-one to ninety-two, says: “Until, when he reached the rising of the sun, he found it rising on a people for whom We had made no shelter against it. Thus indeed it was. Verily, We encompassed with Our knowledge all that was with him.” After securing the western border of his empire, Cyrus advanced to the eastern border. There he fought the Massagetae Scythians, a harsh nomadic people who lived in what is today Uzbekistan and Turkmenistan, east of the Caspian Sea, across today’s Kyzylkum–Karakum desert belt. As nomads they were exposed to the unrelenting sun of Central Asia — just as the Quran describes, a people with no shelter against the rising sun. And not only this: Herodotus records that they worshipped the sun too, sacrificing animals to it. The timeline places these events around five hundred and forty-five to five hundred and forty BCE, when Cyrus established Pasargadae as his capital in Fars and then turned his attention eastward; and around five hundred and forty-five to five hundred and thirty-nine BCE, when he conducted sustained campaigns in Central Asia to secure the empire’s northeastern frontier. Now notice the Quran’s enigmatic statement after mentioning this Eastern expedition: that Allah knows fully all things. This hint indicates that some point of contention arises from the eastern journey. And so it is: some accounts claim he died after these battles. The Quran, however, situates Cyrus’ Eastern journey as only the second of three campaigns, indicating that he lived to fight on. The Greek historians give at least three differing accounts of his death. Herodotus, having explicitly chosen the story that best fits his theme of moral hubris, recounts that Cyrus was beheaded in the Eastern expedition by the tribal queen Tomyris, in fulfilment of an oath she had sworn to the sun-god. Ctesias claims Cyrus fought against a group of the Massagetae, was injured, and died later after returning home, in Persia. But Xenophon reports that Cyrus was victorious against the Massagetae and lived out a long, full life, dying years later. The Quran aligns with Xenophon’s account, and continues to narrate his story after his supposed death. And Cyrus’ well-known tomb, the stepped stone monument pictured here, stands at Pasargadae in south-western Iran — over two thousand miles away from the Massagetae — bearing silent witness that he did not die on the eastern front.
cyrus-confronts-gog-and-magog: Cyrus Confronts Gog and Magog. Here we follow Dhul-Qarnain’s northward journey. The Holy Quran, chapter eighteen, verses ninety-three to ninety-five, tells us: “Then he followed another way. Until, when he reached the place between the two barriers, he found beneath them a people who would scarcely understand a word. They said, ‘O Dhu’l Qarnain, verily, Gog and Magog are creating disorder in the earth; shall we then pay thee tribute on condition that thou set up a barrier between us and them?’” From the East, Dhul-Qarnain travels to a place between two barriers — in Arabic, as-saddayn — beneath which he found a people who could not understand a word of his language. They implored him for help against the tribes of Gog and Magog, who were creating disorder in their land. To identify this people who beseeched Cyrus, we are given three clues. First, they live beneath the shadow of two mountains, or two great barriers. Second, they did not naturally speak his language. And third, they were being attacked by Gog and Magog. When we look at Cyrus’ Achaemenid Empire, at its borders and towards the north, we see the Caucasus, with great towering mountains ranging from the Black Sea to the Caspian Sea. The people below these mountains were the ancestors of today’s Georgians. Their language belonged to the Kartvelian language family, known as a ‘language isolate’, meaning that it is unrelated to any other language family — Cyrus would not have understood them. And who were their attackers? Was there really evidence that the Georgians were historically attacked by tribes who could be called Gog and Magog? Indeed there was. The Scythians were a fierce, battle-hardened, horse-riding archer people who penetrated down from modern Dagestan and Chechnya into Georgia and northern Iran. They descended principally from the Yamnaya people of the Russian steppe. The Yamnaya were an admixture of two major groups: Eastern European hunter-gatherers, and Caucasus hunter-gatherers. From around three thousand BCE the Yamnaya spread both east and west, mixing with local groups over many centuries. In the West, many of their descendants settled into Europe, becoming the Celts; others eventually became the Slavic and Russian peoples.
the-wall-of-cyrus: The Wall of Cyrus the Great. This board tells how Dhul-Qarnain built his mighty barrier — and how history bears it out. First, on the matter of tribute. The Holy Quran, chapter eighteen, verses ninety-five to ninety-six, records the exchange: “‘O Dhu’l Qarnain, verily, Gog and Magog are creating disorder in the earth; shall we then pay thee tribute on condition that thou set up a barrier between us and them?’ He replied, ‘The power with which my Lord has endowed me about this is better, but you may help me with physical strength; I will set up between you and them a rampart.’” Dhul-Qarnain refuses their money and asks only for their labour. Remarkably, it is recorded of the Colchians, who lived in present-day western Georgia, in the words of Herodotus, Book three, ninety-seven: “The following moreover had no tribute fixed for them to pay, but brought gifts, namely…the Colchians… continued to bring the gifts which they had fixed for themselves every four years even down to my own time, that is to say, a hundred boys and a hundred maidens.” No tax, just manpower — the Quran stands vindicated. Now to the building of the wall itself. The Quran, chapter eighteen, verse ninety-seven, continues: “‘Bring me blocks of iron.’ They did so till, when he had levelled up the space between the two mountain sides, he said, ‘Now blow with your bellows.’ They blew till, when he had made it red as fire, he said, ‘Bring me molten qitr that I may pour it thereon.’” The word qitr, though generally understood to mean ‘molten copper’, can also mean molten metal generally. And remarkably, at Pasargadae, Cyrus’ royal centre, there is strong evidence that this was precisely how the builders joined blocks without mortar: by cutting grooves within the stones, placing iron clamps in the grooves, and pouring molten lead over them to secure the joints against rust. As for where the wall was needed, there were two routes of attack open to the Scythians. One was the Darial Gorge, through the Caucasus mountains. The other went around the mountains, between the end of the Caucasus range and the Caspian Sea. Both were guarded by great fortifications built by the Persians to block northern invaders. By the sea stood the Wall of Derbent; and the Darial Gorge, too, had a great wall and fortification system built by the Persians. Either, or both, may have originally been built by Cyrus himself to keep out the Scythians.
the-reality-of-faith: The Reality of Faith. This board closes our section on Cyrus with a single verse, from the Holy Quran, chapter forty-nine, verse fourteen: “O mankind, We have created you from male and female; and We have divided you into tribes and sub-tribes for the sake of easy recognition. Verily, the most honoured among you in the sight of Allah is he who is the most righteous among you. Surely, Allah is All-Knowing, All-Aware.” Here is the reality of faith. Our division into nations and tribes, the Quran teaches, was never a hierarchy — it is only for the sake of easy recognition. True nobility lies not in birth or lineage, but in righteousness — the very quality that marked Cyrus the Great, a Persian king raised up to become the saviour of another people.
the-rise-of-islam: The Rise of Islam. You are entering the second part of our journey, which this board also calls the Second Persian Revolution. Here, in the boards ahead, we leave the age of Cyrus and come to the deserts of Arabia, where a new light was about to break upon a world that had sunk into darkness. Before you stands the Ka'bah at Mecca, the ancient House of God, with the minarets of a great mosque rising behind it. This is the setting for the story of how Islam arose, and of the remarkable place that Persia and its people would come to hold within it. Let us begin in an age of darkness.
an-age-of-darkness: An Age of Darkness. Before you a dark sea breaks against the shore under a deep blue night, a fitting image for a world in decay. As the Holy Quran says, in chapter thirty, verse forty-two: "Corruption has appeared on land and sea because of what men's hands have wrought, that He may make them taste the fruit of some of their doings, so that they may turn back from evil." By the seventh century, Persia was no longer the empire of Cyrus the Great. The Achaemenid Empire had lasted for over two centuries, broadly keeping its tolerant Zoroastrian spirit. But in around three hundred and thirty BCE it was destroyed by Alexander the Great, and though three Persian empires followed, Zoroastrianism itself changed: the one supreme God was replaced by multiple spirits, fire and sun-worship grew, and the Zoroastrian priestly class began to persecute minorities. Yet the Persians were not alone in their corruption. For centuries they were locked in conflict with the Romans, whose rulers brutalised non-orthodox Christians and impoverished their peoples with taxation, so that deep corruption bred resentment from within. Both the Persians and the Romans held client Arab states beneath them. The Persians ruled Yemen as a province and controlled the Lakhmids of the Levant; and in around six hundred CE, the Sasanian king Khoswrow the Second murdered al-Nu'man the Third, the Arab king of the Lakhmids, causing uproar among the Arab tribes. The world of Arabia itself was little different: inter-tribal warfare raged, baby girls were sometimes killed at birth, and slavery was rife and brutal. And yet it was in this very world that something new emerged. In the words of Hazrat Mirza Ghulam Ahmad: "Have you any notion what was the strange event that occurred in the desert country of Arabia when hundreds of thousands of the dead were revived within a brief period and those who had been misguided through generations put on Divine colour, and those who were blind obtained sight, and those who had been dumb began to speak of the understanding of the Divine, and the world underwent a revolution which had never been seen or heard of before? It was the supplications during dark nights of one who had lost himself in God which raised a clamour in the world, and manifested such wonders as appeared impossible in the case of that unlearned helpless one. Send down Thy blessings and peace, O Allah, on him and his people according to the amount of pain and anguish he felt for his Ummah, and pour down upon him the lights of Thy mercy forever."
a-new-dawn: A New Dawn. Before you a glowing sunrise rises over the sea, the dawn of a new age. The Prophet Muhammad, peace and blessings be upon him, was born in Mecca in five hundred and seventy CE, into a noble family, but he lost his father, mother and grandfather at a young age. Overseen by his uncle, he became a young man renowned for his piety and trustworthiness. At the age of twenty-five he married Khadija, a forty-year-old merchant-woman, and he spent much of his time in the Cave of Hira, praying for days on end to God Almighty. In six hundred and ten CE, he began to receive revelations from Allah. This Quranic revelation continued for some twenty-three lunar years until his demise in six hundred and thirty-two CE, and the core of the message was simple: "There is no God but Allah, and Muhammad is His Messenger." It came at a time when the unity of God had been lost, giving way to Christian Trinitarianism and to various forms of polytheism, and Islam restored pure unity to the world. As the Holy Quran declares, in chapter two, verse two hundred and fifty-six: "Allah is He save Whom none is worthy of worship, the Ever-Living, the Self-Subsisting and All-Sustaining. Slumber seizes Him not, nor sleep. To Him belongs whatsoever is in the heavens and whatsoever is in the earth." These Islamic beliefs were beautifully set out one day in the presence of the Prophet himself, in a narration related by Umar ibn Khattab, in the collection of Sahih Muslim: "We were sitting one day with the Holy Prophet when a man appeared among us whose clothes were of an intense whiteness, whose hair was very black, who bore no mark of travel and who was not known to any of us. He sat down in front of the Holy Prophet, their knees touching, and placing his hands on his thighs he said: Muhammad, tell me about Islam. The Holy Prophet said: Islam is that you should bear witness that there is no one worthy of worship save Allah alone and that Muhammad is His Messenger, and that you should observe Prayer, pay the Zakat, observe the fast during Ramadhan, and perform the Pilgrimage to the House if you can afford the journey thither. The man said: That is right. We were surprised that he inquired and also confirmed the correctness of the answer. He then said: Tell me about faith. The Holy Prophet said: That you should believe in Allah, His Angels, His Books, His Messengers, the Last Day, and that you should believe that He determines the measure of good and evil. The man said: That is right. Now tell me about the due performance of obligations. The Holy Prophet said: That you should worship Allah as if you are beholding Him, and if not then in the consciousness that He is watching you. Then the man departed, and I remained a while. The Holy Prophet said to me: Umar, do you know who the questioner was? I said: Allah and His Messenger know best. He said: It was Gabriel who came to instruct you in your faith."
the-prophecy-of-rome: The Prophecy of Rome. Before you is the weathered stone bust of a Roman emperor. In these early years the Prophet Muhammad, peace and blessings be upon him, and the first believers were heavily persecuted for their belief in Allah, the one true God; many were tortured, and many martyred. For over a decade the persecution intensified, until in six hundred and twenty-two CE the Arab tribes united to murder the Holy Prophet, but God informed him of the plot. He escaped from Mecca to Medina, over two hundred miles away. Yet the persecution did not end, and eventually a state of war was thrust upon the Muslims. During this same period, Khoswrow the Second, the Persian emperor, led a stunning military campaign against the Roman emperor Heraclius. The Romans lost Damascus in six hundred and thirteen CE, and Jerusalem, along with the True Cross of Christ, in six hundred and fourteen CE. Rome seemed finished. But in six hundred and sixteen CE came a remarkable prophecy, in the Holy Quran, chapter thirty, verses three to five: "The Romans have been defeated, In the land nearby, and they, after their defeat, will be victorious. In a few years, Allah's is the command before and after that, and on that day the believers will rejoice." This foretold three things at once: that the Romans would be victorious over the Persians again; that the victory would come, in the Arabic word bid'un, within three to nine years; and that the Muslims would be especially glad on that day. In six hundred and twenty-four CE, a Meccan army of strong, seasoned warriors was sent out against the Prophet and his followers, and in the Valley of Badr the great battle would take place. The disparity was stark. The Meccans fielded one thousand men, seven hundred camels and one hundred horses; the Muslims, only three hundred and thirteen men, seventy camels and two horses. The night before, the Prophet was informed of victory, and so it was. And incredibly, it was on this very day that the Muslims learned that the Romans had indeed defeated the Persians; for Heraclius' counter-offensive campaign, beginning in six hundred and twenty-two, had reached great success in six hundred and twenty-four, defeating three Persian armies in succession. Thus all three arms of the prophecy were fulfilled: the Romans were victorious; the victory came within nine years; and the Muslims too were glad, victorious both in battle and in prophecy.
the-persian-companion: The Persian Companion. Palm trees rise before a mosque here, a fitting image for Salman Al-Farsi, Salman the Persian. Born in Isfahan, in modern-day Iran, Salman was raised as a Magian firekeeper, that is, a Zoroastrian. He once came across a Christian church, and was so impressed by their worship that, upon telling his father of his desire to convert, he was shackled in the house. After escaping to Syria, he travelled the lands, searching for spiritual teachers who adhered to true righteousness. But on his death bed, his last teacher advised that there were none like him left. He did, however, foretell that a new Prophet was coming to the Arabs, and gave Salman three marks of his Prophethood to test him with, saying that he would come to a land between two bare tracts, populated by palm trees. Salman now had his next destination. He found his way to Arabia, but was betrayed and sold into slavery. Eventually he reached Medina, a land of palm trees, and waited. One day, he heard that a man claiming to be a Prophet was coming to Medina. He rushed to see if he fulfilled the three marks: distributing but not accepting charity, eating of a gift, and bearing a particular mark on his back. Upon testing him, he discovered that the Prophet was true; and from then on, Salman would never leave the Prophet in a time of need. Though he had begun his life as a Magian, Salman played a pivotal role in protecting his Muslim brethren from the idolaters. As the Holy Quran teaches, in chapter twenty-two, verse eighteen: "As to those who believe, and the Jews, and the Sabians, and the Christians, and the Magians and the idolaters, verily, Allah will judge between them on the Day of Resurrection; surely, Allah is Witness over all things." When the Muslims were being attacked by a huge army, Salman used his knowledge of non-Arab warfare to suggest digging a protective trench on the exposed side of Medina. This approach, never seen before by the Arabs, worked, and the Muslims were victorious against the idolaters once more. And when two groups of the companions argued as to whether Salman was of the Emigrants, who left Mecca, or of the Helpers, who were from Medina, the Prophet, peace and blessings be upon him, intervened, saying: "Salman is from among us, that is, the People of the Prophet's Household."
faith-in-the-pleiades: Faith in the Pleiades. This board records a single, luminous moment, narrated by Abu Huraira, in the collection of Bukhari. He relates: "One Day we were sitting with the Holy Prophet when Surah Jumu'ah was revealed. I asked the Holy Prophet, 'Who are the people to whom the words And among others from among them who have not yet joined them, refer?' Salman the Persian was sitting among us. Upon my repeatedly asking him the same question, the Prophet put his hand on Salman and said, 'If Faith were to go up to the Pleiades, a man from among these would surely find it.'" It is a promise that, as this exhibition unfolds, points across the centuries to a reformer of Persian descent.
persian-downfall: Persian Downfall. Before you, a row of carved Persian heads gazes out from Persepolis. The Prophet Muhammad, peace and blessings be upon him, was commissioned to pronounce his Prophethood to the world, and so he wrote letters to both Heraclius and Khoswrow the Second, inviting them to Islam. Heraclius treated both the message and the emissary who brought it with great respect. Khoswrow, however, was incensed when he read the letter, and tore it to pieces, saying: "He is my servant and he addresses me in this way!" When the Prophet heard how the message of Allah had been treated, he proclaimed: "Now these people shall themselves be shattered to pieces." His letter to Persia had been a message of peace. It read: "I write this letter in the name of Allah, the Gracious, who grants the best reward of our deeds. This letter is from Muhammad, the Messenger of Allah addressed to the Ruler of Persia. Peace be on him who accepts the guidance and believes in Allah and His Messenger and bears witness that there is none worthy of worship except God, He has no partner; And also bears witness that Muhammad is His Servant and Messenger. O Ruler of Persia! I invite you to the call of Allah, as I am the messenger of Allah to the whole of mankind; so that I may warn every living person and so that the judgement of God may overtake the disbelievers. O Ruler of Persia! Accept Islam, for this is the only way to your salvation. However, if you turn away, then remember that the sin of your Zoroastrian people shall also be on your shoulders." But Khoswrow answered with an assassination. He ordered the Governor of Yemen to send two men to arrest the Holy Prophet: he was to give allegiance to the Persian Emperor, or face death. When the emissaries reached the Prophet and gave their demands, the Holy Prophet asked them to stay the night. In the morning, he told them: "Tell your master that my Lord, the God of power, has killed his master last night." When the emissaries returned to Yemen, they told the Governor the prophecy. And within days they received word: Khoswrow the Second had been murdered by his own son, and the murder had taken place on the very night the prophecy was revealed. Though neither Heraclius nor Khoswrow accepted Islam, one gave respect to the message while the other tore it to pieces. And for this reason, the Prophet said: "The Persian Kingdom shall be destroyed at once, but God shall grant some respite to the Roman Empire." And so it was.
islamic-tolerance: Islamic Tolerance. The domes and minarets of a great mosque stand silhouetted against the sunset before you. Islam came both to unite and to protect, and the Holy Quran itself proclaims freedom of conscience. In chapter eighteen, verse thirty, it declares: "Proclaim: This is the truth from your Lord; then let him who will, believe, and let him who will, disbelieve." And in chapter two, verse two hundred and fifty-seven: "There is no compulsion in religion." Consider first Arab unity. After the demise of the Prophet Muhammad, peace and blessings be upon him, in six hundred and thirty-two, he was succeeded by the Rightly-Guided Caliphs. Arabia was for the first time unified. It entered battle with both the Romans and the Persians, and shocked the world by securing victory over them. Yet with that victory came a remarkable religious respect. Governance of the Persian capital was given to none other than Salman Al-Farsi. Muslim rule, especially in its first three centuries, was tolerant and protective of its religious minorities; the spirit of Cyrus was enlarged and perfected under Islam. For instance, in the tenth century, fire-temples were still to be found in almost every province of Persia. In Muslim Spain, the Jews had been greatly persecuted by the Christian Visigoths; under Muslim rule, they experienced a cultural renaissance that lasted for centuries. The tenth-century Andalusian Caliph Abd Al-Rahman the Third even had a Jewish physician as his foreign minister, and a Christian bishop as his envoy. But why did so many embrace the new faith? Sir Thomas Arnold, in his book The Preaching of Islam, published in eighteen ninety-six, explains how conversions to Islam were organic and gradual in newly Muslim lands. Yet many Persians were particularly swift in embracing Islam. As he writes: "… the Muslim creed was most eagerly welcomed by the townsfolk, the industrial classes and the artisans, whose occupations made them impure according to the Zoroastrian creed, because in the pursuance of their trade or occupations they defiled fire, earth or water, and who thus, outcasts in the eyes of the law and treated with scant consideration in consequence, embraced with eagerness a creed that made them at once free men, and equal in a brotherhood of faith… For the Persian could find in the Qur'an many of the fundamental doctrines of his old faith, though in a rather different form: he would meet again Ahuramazda and Ahriman under the names of Allah and Iblis; the creation of the world in six periods; the angels and the demons; the story of the primitive innocence of man; the resurrection of the body and the doctrine of heaven and hell. Even in the details of daily worship there were similarities to be found and the followers of Zoroaster when they adopted Islam were enjoined by their new faith to pray five times a day just as they had been by the Avesta."
persian-renaissance: Persian Renaissance. Above you glows a turquoise-tiled Persian dome. Consider first the Persian Muslims. Successive Persian empires spread far beyond the province of Fars and the borders of modern Iran. They covered parts of Arabia, Central Asia, the Levant, Afghanistan, the Indian subcontinent, and the Caucasus. All these peoples were influenced by Persian culture, language and administration; thus, many were considered Persianate without being ethnically Iranian. These peoples embraced Islam wholesale, and Persian became the language of scholarship and elite culture in many parts of the Islamic world. Consider next the matter of Sunni and Shia. Though today Iran is associated with Shiism, this was not the case for much of its history. Before the sixteenth century, most Iranians were Sunni. When the Safavid dynasty made Twelver Shiism the state religion in fifteen hundred and one, Sunni Islam was suppressed, and Shiism then became entrenched in Iran and much of nearby Azerbaijan. Most of the rest of the historically Persianate lands remained majority-Sunni. Yet both Sunni and Shia Muslims believed in the advent of the Imam Mahdi — a great reformer of Islam in the Latter Days. As we shall see, this concept is deeply linked to the past and future of Persia in Islam. Persians contributed considerably to the cause of Islam, and a small sample of them stands before you. There is Imam Abu Hanifa, founder of the Hanafi school of Islamic jurisprudence, of the eighth century, in modern Iraq. Imam Bukhari, the leading compiler of ahadith, of the ninth century, in modern Uzbekistan. Imam Muslim, another leading compiler of ahadith, of the ninth century, in modern Iran. Abu Bakr Al-Razi, a leading Islamic physician and shaper of modern medical culture, of the ninth and tenth centuries, in modern Iran. Al-Khwarizmi, the father of algebra and transmitter of the Hindu-Arabic numeral system, of the eighth and ninth centuries, in modern Uzbekistan. Al-Farabi, a leading Muslim philosopher, of the tenth century, in modern Kazakhstan. Al-Biruni, a polymath thinker and the author of an authoritative account of Indian ethnography, of the tenth and eleventh centuries, in modern Uzbekistan. Ibn Sina, the defining leading physician of the Muslim world and a defining philosophical mind, of the tenth and eleventh centuries, in modern Uzbekistan. Al-Ghazali, a scholar-saint who blended Sufism with Islamic orthodoxy, of the eleventh and twelfth centuries, in modern Iran. Omar Khayyam, polymath scientist, thinker and poet, of the eleventh and twelfth centuries, in modern Iran. Sheikh Abdul Qadir al-Jilani, a Sufi founder and Saint of Islam, from modern Iran. And Jalal Al-Din Rumi, the great Sufi saint and poet, of the thirteenth century, from modern Central Asia and Turkey. The renaissance of Persia became a gift to the whole world.
o-sons-of-persia: O Sons of Persia. Before you is a short but stirring revelation given to Hazrat Mirza Ghulam Ahmad, peace be upon him. Twice it repeats a single command, and then names those it addresses: "Hold fast to unity. Hold fast to unity. O sons of Persia." It is a call across the centuries to the people of Persia, urging them to hold together in faith. As this final section will reveal, that call was bound up with the coming of a reformer of Persian origin, the Promised Messiah, to whom we now turn.
the-promised-messiah: The Promised Messiah. You are entering the final section of Prophecy and Persia: The Promised Messiah, the Imam Mahdi of Persian origin. Before you is a portrait of a bearded man in a turban: Hazrat Mirza Ghulam Ahmad. Having journeyed from Cyrus of Persia, through the rise of Islam and Persia's place within it, we come at last to the awaited reformer of Persian descent. Let the final part of our story begin.
a-new-dark-age: A New Dark Age. Above you hangs a slender crescent moon against a black sky, for by the nineteenth century a new darkness had fallen upon Islam. The Prophet Muhammad, peace and blessings be upon him, had foretold such a time. He warned, as recorded in Mishkat Al-Masabih: "There will come a time upon the people when nothing will remain of Islam except its name only and nothing will remain of the Quran except its inscription. Their mosques will be splendidly furnished but destitute of guidance. Their divines will be the worst people under the heaven and strife will issue from them, and avert to them." The Holy Quran itself, in chapter twenty-five, verse thirty-one, foretells this abandonment: "And the Messenger will say, 'O my Lord, my people indeed treated this Qur'an as a discarded thing.'" Yet in the same age of decline there was also a promise of renewal, for the Prophet had asked, as recorded in Sahih Al Bukhari: "How will you be when the Son of Mary will descend upon you, and he will be the Imam from among you?" Consider now how that dark age had come upon Islam in India. In the nineteenth century, Islam was under deadly attack. For centuries, the Muslim states had given way to Christian empires. In British India, Christian missionaries were converting Muslims by the thousand, pointing to the mainstream belief that Jesus was alive in heaven, while the Prophet Muhammad had passed away. And a new Hindu group, the Arya Samaj, was undermining the Holy Prophet of Islam and the Quran. Islam was crying out for a Saviour. One man came forth. His name was Mirza Ghulam Ahmad of Qadian. He described the final struggle that this awaited Messiah would face, writing: "This Messiah who on account of the promise of God concerning his appearance is called the Promised Messiah in sacred books, would, it is foretold, be made to stand against Satan, and between him and the evil one there would be a mighty struggle, the final struggle between good and evil. For this spiritual fight Satan would bring all his hosts into the field and gather together all his power and make use of all his resources. Never shall a fight like this have occurred between good and evil before, for on that day the tricks of Satan, and the means by which he can mislead people, would all be exhausted. After a heavy fight, the Messiah of God would drive back the powers of darkness, and the glory, majesty, unity and holiness of God would be proclaimed upon earth and would continue to be declared for a thousand years, the seventh day of the Holy Books of God. Then will be the end. I am that Messiah: let him who will, accept me."
the-persian-messiah: The Persian Messiah. Before you rises an ornate Mughal-style palace facade, a fitting backdrop for the story of a reformer of Persian origin. Consider first the ascent of faith. The Holy Quran, in chapter sixty-two, verse four, declares: "He it is Who has raised among the Unlettered people a Messenger from among themselves who recites unto them His Signs, and purifies them, and teaches them the Book and wisdom, although they were before, in manifest misguidance. And among others from among them who have not yet joined them. He is the Mighty, the Wise." A companion related: "One Day we were sitting with the Holy Prophet when Surah Jumu'ah was revealed," and the Prophet put his hand on Salman the Persian and said, as recorded in Bukhari: "If Faith were to go up to the Pleiades, a man from these would surely find it." Now consider Ahmad the Persian. The family of Hazrat Ahmad, who lived from eighteen thirty-five to nineteen hundred and eight, was of Persian origin. His ancestors settled in a region of modern Uzbekistan called Samarkand, a land that had been ruled by Persian empires and had deeply entrenched Persianate culture. He received many revelations indicating his Persian origin, among them: "A man of Persian descent has published the refutation of those who have disbelieved and have obstructed people from the path of Allah. Allah appreciates his effort. The book of the friend of Allah is the sword of 'Ali." And so we come to the champion of Allah. Born in eighteen thirty-five into a family of local chieftains, Ahmad spent his early life in solitude, studying Islamic, Christian and Hindu scriptures, and sinking deep into prayer. Though he sought to remain unknown, he eventually felt compelled to defend Islam publicly against the many attacks it was enduring. In the eighteen-eighties he released a magnificent defence of Islam entitled Barahin-e-Ahmadiyya. About this work, the cleric who would one day become his bitterest enemy wrote: "In our opinion, from the point of view of the modern age, this book stands unique in the history of Islam. No book has ever been published like it in the past, and we cannot say anything about the future, which is known only to God. The perseverance of the author in the service of Islam, through his life, his energy, pen, tongue, and every form of activity is almost unprecedented amongst Muslims. This should not be taken as Asiatic exaggeration. We challenge anyone to show us the like of this book."
prophecy-of-the-promised-messiah: A Prophecy Fulfilled. This board sets before you a single reflection in the words of Hazrat Mirza Ghulam Ahmad, framed within a golden arch. He writes: "Our lord and master the Holy Prophet, may peace and blessings of Allah be upon him and his progeny, had declared as a prophecy that a time would come when the Muslims would acquire a strong resemblance to the Jews. Then a man of Persian origin would be raised to teach them the faith. Had faith ascended to the Pleiades, that man would have brought it down from that height. This is a prophecy of the Holy Prophet, may peace and blessings of Allah be upon him. Divine revelation has expounded the purport of this prophecy to my humble self and God the Almighty has made it clear to me by revelation that Hadrat Masih Ibn-e-Maryam, the Messiah, Son of Mary, was also a teacher of faith who appeared fourteen centuries after Hadrat Musa, that is Moses, peace be upon him. Now, it so happened that when about fourteen hundred years had passed after the advent of the Holy Prophet, on whom be the peace and blessings of Allah, the Muslims largely developed the same evil characteristics which the Jews had developed. Thus, it was necessary to fulfil the prophecy made about them. In consequence, God the Almighty, of His perfect power, has sent for them the like of the Messiah as a teacher of faith."
gog-and-magog: Gog and Magog. As you saw earlier, when Dhul-Qarnain raised his great wall, the Holy Quran issued a warning. It reads, in chapter eighteen, verses ninety-nine and one hundred: "'This is a mercy from my Lord. But when the promise of my Lord shall come to pass, He will break it into pieces. And the promise of my Lord is certainly true.' And on that day We shall leave some of them to surge against others, and the trumpet will be blown. Then shall We gather them all together." As we have seen, Gog and Magog were violent tribes invading from Europe and Russia into the lands of the Persians and Arabs. This verse prophesied that the walls would not hold forever; and so it was to be. In eighteen hundred and one, Russia took the land of the Darial Gorge. In eighteen thirteen, the Derbent fortifications fell to the Russians too. They would use these entry points to attack Iran for over a hundred years. The warning was fulfilled. But who are Gog and Magog, in truth? Here the Promised Messiah, Hazrat Mirza Ghulam Ahmad, unveils them as masters of fire. In his own words: "Since Ajij, from which the words Gog and Magog are derived, means 'fire', God Almighty has disclosed to me that Gog and Magog are a people who are greater experts in the use of fire than any other people. Their very names indicate that their ships, trains and machines will be run by fire. They will fight their battles with fire. They will excel all other people in harnessing fire to their service. This is why they will be called Gog and Magog. These are the people of the West, as they are unique in their expertise in the use of fire." And there is a spiritual dimension too, which he called the Dajjal. The greatest trial foretold of these Latter Days was that of the Dajjal, the Antichrist civilisation overspreading the world. Gog and Magog referred to their material aspects, while Dajjal referred to their spiritual aspects. As Hazrat Ahmad explained: "Dajjal in fact is none other than the people known as Christian missionaries and European philosophers. They act like the two jaws of the Promised Dajjal with which he devours people's faith like a python. First it is the common and ignorant people who get caught in the wiles of the missionaries; and then, those who happen to escape their clutches being disgusted with the disgraceful and false beliefs, are caught in the net of the European philosophers. I see that the common people are more vulnerable to the lies of the clergy, whereas the intellectual ones are more susceptible to the falsehood spread by the philosophers."
dhul-qarnain-revealed: Dhul-Qarnain. Is this a story of the past, or of the future? The Quran is full of secrets. One secret revealed by the Promised Messiah is this: that historical events narrated by the Quran foretell future events. And in the same way that the story of Dhul-Qarnain referred to the great Persian Emperor of the past, it also referred to the Promised Persian Messiah of the future. We will now examine how the verses of the Dhul-Qarnain story apply to Hazrat Ahmad, peace be upon him. Consider first the very meaning of the name. In his own words: "I have come as Dhul-Qarnain for this Ummah. It is evident that Dhul-Qarnain has to be a person who witnesses two centuries. And, it is a marvellous matter about me that when you look at all the calendars in use, you will find that I have lived in two centuries according to the calendars of all people." Consider, too, how he was established in the earth. He wrote: "I have been provided with all the means for the propagation of the truth which were not even available at the time of any other prophet. A piece of writing can now be published throughout the world within forty days, whereas previously a man could not achieve this even in a hundred years." Now recall the two great journeys of Dhul-Qarnain. First, the westward journey, to the setting of the sun. As the Promised Messiah explained: "These verses contain a prophecy that the Promised Messiah will appear at a time when the people of the West will be steeped in darkness. The Sun of Truth will completely disappear from their view, going down into a dirty, stinking pool; in other words, instead of truth, foul beliefs and practices will be prevalent among them. This would be their water which they will drink. They will have no light whatsoever and will wallow in darkness. And as is clear, this is exactly the condition of the Christian faith today as described by the Holy Quran, and the great centre of Christianity is also in the Western countries." And then the eastward journey, to the rising of the sun. He explained: "That is, Dhul-Qarnain will observe the state of the people of the East, and will discover a people at the place of the rising of the Sun of Truth who will be so ignorant that they will have no means of protecting themselves from the glare of the sun. That is, they will be scorched by their worship of external form and extremism, and they will be unaware of the truth. In this way, the rising Sun of Truth will bring about their destruction."
the-wall-of-islam: The Wall of Islam. The Promised Messiah described how Dhul-Qarnain would reach a third and critical way, a place between two barriers. In his words: "Dhul-Qarnain, the Promised Messiah, would then follow another course and would find himself at a very critical situation that can be described as being between two barriers or mountains. This means that he would encounter a time when people on either side would be in fear, and the powers of darkness, in collaboration with the powers of state, would present an awe-striking spectacle. Under both these powers he would discover a people who would find it difficult to understand him. They would be the victims of false beliefs on account of which they would find it difficult to understand the guidance which he would present to them, but in the end they would understand him and would attain guidance. They are the third kind of people who would benefit from the guidance of the Promised Messiah." To this people he gave a charge, to build a wall of iron. Recall from the Quran the words, 'Bring me slabs of iron.' The Promised Messiah explained their inner meaning: "That is, he would put forth such conclusive proofs and arguments that their enemies would not be left with any basis for criticism or objection against their religion. 'Bring me slabs of iron so that their movement can be stopped'; that is, hold fast to my teachings and my arguments, fully adopt steadfastness, and build yourselves like unto a wall of iron to repel the enemy's onslaught. 'Then blow fire into the iron until it becomes like fire'; that is, feed the flames of love for God until you yourselves assume the complexion of the Divine." And what would this community become? He said: "That is, the God of the Heavens will raise the Promised Messiah and create a third people for whom He will show great Signs of His support until all rightly inclined people are gathered upon one faith; that is to say, upon Islam. They will hear the voice of the Messiah and will hasten towards him; then will there be only one Shepherd and one flock." So the parallel is complete. What Cyrus the Great fulfilled in stone and conquest, Hazrat Ahmad fulfilled in spirit. Cyrus unified two kingdoms into one; expanded early eastward into the Turkish lands of muddy springs; expanded later westward into Central Asia; and built fortifications against the invading Scythians. The Promised Messiah joined two centuries in each calendar; brought the spiritual light of Islam to western peoples devoid of spirituality; fought against the extremism and literalism of eastern peoples; and founded a Community to act as a barrier against the Dajjal.
the-palace-prophecy: The Palace Prophecy. On the fifteenth of January, nineteen hundred and six, the Promised Messiah received a striking revelation, later published in Badr on the nineteenth of January that same year: "The Palace of Chosroes has been shaken." This momentous prophecy concerned the modern rulers of Iran, the Qajar Monarchy. Until this revelation, the Qajar monarchy had enjoyed over a century of absolute imperial rule. The Shah had the full military and diplomatic support of Imperial Russia. Muzaffarud-Din Shah, the Shah at the time, had recently accepted some constitutional reforms, and this revelation indicated that a fundamental change in Iranian history was about to take place. Then came the coup. After the Shah's death in nineteen hundred and seven, conflict intensified between his successor, Mohammad Ali Shah, and the Iranian Parliament. In nineteen hundred and eight, the new Shah suspended the constitutional order and had Cossack forces shell the parliament building in Tehran. His crackdown briefly restored royal rule. However, pro-constitutional forces organised armed resistance. In nineteen hundred and nine, they captured Tehran and forced the Shah to abdicate in favour of his young son. The monarchy limped on before ending in nineteen twenty-five. The palace of Chosroes had truly been shaken. Alongside this stood a revelation about the uniting of Muslims. Received on the twentieth of November, nineteen hundred and five, it showed the unifying purpose of his advent: "Gather together all the Muslims on the face of the earth so that they should unite in one faith." And in a Persian revelation of the eleventh of March, nineteen hundred and six, he was told: "When the reign of the Messiah, the monarch begins, the Muslims who were Muslims in name only will be reconverted to Islam." He explained its meaning: "In this revelation, by the reign of the just monarch is meant the period of the call of this humble one. No worldly kingdom is indicated here, but the kingdom of heaven that has been bestowed upon me. The meaning of this revelation is that when the kingdom of heaven, which in the estimation of Allah is the period of the Promised Messiah, the effect of it was that those who were only Muslims in name began to be Muslims in fact, as has already happened in the case of about four hundred thousand of them."
a-blessed-dream: A Blessed Dream. We close our journey with a blessed dream of Hazrat Mirza Ghulam Ahmad, the Promised Messiah, peace be upon him. He relates: "I saw in a blessed dream a group of sincere believers and just and righteous kings, some of whom belonged to this country, some to Arabia, some to Iran, some to Syria, some to Turkey, and some to other regions of which I am not aware." Thereafter, he says, he was told by Allah the Almighty: "These people will affirm your righteousness and will believe in you and will call down blessings upon you and will pray for you. I shall bestow great blessings upon you, so much so that kings will seek blessings from your garments and I will join them amongst your sincere followers." And so our path comes to its end, having travelled from Cyrus of Persia to the Promised Messiah of Persian origin.
